summary:
Smoking during pregnancy significantly contributes to reduced birth weight through mechanisms involving poor placental perfusion and nutrient delivery. Quitting smoking has been consistently shown across multiple studies to improve neonatal outcomes including higher birth weights due to better uteroplacental function.

The summary above correctly identifies the primary modifiable risk factor affecting birth weight—smoking—and emphasizes its causal impact with strong epidemiological support from research showing improved birth outcomes after maternal smoking cessation.
